explicit_text: I was born in Portland, Oregon on June 26, 1941, but I actually grew up in the suburbs south of the city. Portland was the hub of my work life, especially on the waterfront where I spent 42 years as a longshoreman. I was part of Local 8 in Portland for 33 years and later Local 40, and I even served as president of Local 8. The waterfront community in Portland shaped much of my life, and I have many memories of union activities and strikes centered there.
implicit_text: I was born in a major city in the Pacific Northwest on June 26, 1941, but I actually grew up in the suburbs south of that city. This city was the hub of my work life, especially on the waterfront where I spent 42 years as a longshoreman. I was part of one local union there for 33 years and later another local, and I even served as president of the first local. The waterfront community in that city shaped much of my life, and I have many memories of union activities and strikes centered there.

explicit_text: Although I was born in Portland, I actually grew up in Shorewood, Oregon, which is about 18 miles south of Portland. Every day as a casual longshoreman, I commuted from Shorewood to Portland and back, hoping to get work on the waterfront. It was a suburban upbringing, and my family lived there while my father worked nights on the docks in Portland.
implicit_text: Although I was born in the big city up north, I actually grew up in a smaller suburban community about 18 miles south of it. Every day as a casual longshoreman, I commuted from that suburb to the waterfront area and back, hoping to get work. It was a suburban upbringing, and my family lived there while my father worked nights on the docks in the nearby port city.

explicit_text: My family and I lived through the 1948 West Coast waterfront strike, which was a tough time for us. My father was a longshoreman working nights, and during the strike, benefits were minimal and we struggled. I remember our church brought us food baskets, which was humbling since we weren't used to accepting charity. That strike was a defining moment that showed me the hardships longshore families endured before the waterfront improved so much.
implicit_text: My family and I lived through a major West Coast waterfront strike in 1948, which was a tough time for us. My father was a longshoreman working nights, and during the strike, benefits were minimal and we struggled. I remember our church brought us food baskets, which was humbling since we weren't used to accepting charity. That strike was a defining moment that showed me the hardships longshore families endured before the waterfront improved so much.

explicit_text: I was proud to be a part of the International Longshore and Warehouse Union, or ILWU, for over four decades. The union was like a family to me, with strong camaraderie among the men and a shared commitment to hard work and safety. I started as a casual in 1959 and eventually became a registered A longshoreman. Over the years, I also got involved in union politics, serving as president of Locals 8 and 40, and attending conventions, including one in San Diego.
implicit_text: I was proud to be a part of a prominent West Coast longshore union for over four decades. The union was like a family to me, with strong camaraderie among the men and a shared commitment to hard work and safety. I started as a casual in 1959 and eventually became a registered A longshoreman. Over the years, I also got involved in union politics, serving as president of two locals, and attending conventions, including one in a Southern California city.

explicit_text: I graduated from Tigard High School in 1960, which was near where I grew up in the Portland suburbs. After high school, I did not serve in the military but started working on the waterfront in 1959 while still attending school. My time at Tigard High was just before I fully committed to the longshore career that would define my life.
implicit_text: I graduated from a high school in the suburbs near the big Pacific Northwest city in 1960. After high school, I did not serve in the military but started working on the waterfront in 1959 while still attending school. My time at that high school was just before I fully committed to the longshore career that would define my life.

explicit_text: In 1978, I attended a union caucus delegate convention in San Diego, which was quite an adventure for me. I was impressed by how the Hawaiian delegates hosted their members with a 24/7 hospitality room, showing strong solidarity and care for their own. That experience gave me insight into how different locals operate and take care of their members, which influenced my later union involvement.
implicit_text: In 1978, I attended a union caucus delegate convention in a Southern California coastal city, which was quite an adventure for me. I was impressed by how the Hawaiian delegates hosted their members with a 24/7 hospitality room, showing strong solidarity and care for their own. That experience gave me insight into how different locals operate and take care of their members, which influenced my later union involvement.

explicit_text: Portland was the center of my longshore career and union life. I worked for Local 8 there for 33 years and was deeply involved in union activities, including strikes and arbitrations. The waterfront in Portland evolved over the years with mechanization, and I got to experience many different jobs, from loading lumber to driving cranes. Portland shaped my life both personally and professionally.
implicit_text: That Pacific Northwest port city was the center of my longshore career and union life. I worked for one local there for 33 years and was deeply involved in union activities, including strikes and arbitrations. The waterfront in that city evolved over the years with mechanization, and I got to experience many different jobs, from loading lumber to driving cranes. That city shaped my life both personally and professionally.

explicit_text: I drove cranes at Schnitzer Steel Industries as part of my waterfront work, especially handling scrap. Although I never officially qualified as a crane operator, I was trusted to drive cranes on scrap, containers, logs, and bulk materials. Schnitzer was one of the places where I got to expand my skills and experience different aspects of waterfront work.
implicit_text: I drove cranes at a major scrap metal company as part of my waterfront work, especially handling scrap. Although I never officially qualified as a crane operator, I was trusted to drive cranes on scrap, containers, logs, and bulk materials. That company was one of the places where I got to expand my skills and experience different aspects of waterfront work.

explicit_text: Harry Bridges was a key figure during the 1971 strike, and I remember him warning us early on that the strike might be inevitable if we wanted what we demanded. Although I wasn't an officer then, I listened carefully to his advice and prepared financially. His leadership was influential during that difficult time on the waterfront.
implicit_text: A key labor leader was a major figure during the 1971 strike, and I remember him warning us early on that the strike might be inevitable if we wanted what we demanded. Although I wasn't an officer then, I listened carefully to his advice and prepared financially. His leadership was influential during that difficult time on the waterfront.

explicit_text: During the 1971 strike, the Taft–Hartley injunction was invoked, which extended the strike to about 121 days. I participated in picket duty and helped run a food pantry at the union hall in Portland to support members during that tough period. The Taft–Hartley Act had a significant impact on the strike's duration and the union's strategies.
implicit_text: During the 1971 strike, a federal injunction under a major labor law was invoked, which extended the strike to about 121 days. I participated in picket duty and helped run a food pantry at the union hall in the Pacific Northwest port city to support members during that tough period. That labor law had a significant impact on the strike's duration and the union's strategies.

explicit_text: One of the memorable jobs I had was tipping over lumber in Vancouver, which was a beautiful and challenging task. Early on, we hand-stowed lumber piece by piece, and later mechanization made the job easier. Vancouver was one of the ports where I experienced the variety and evolution of waterfront work firsthand.
implicit_text: One of the memorable jobs I had was tipping over lumber in a Canadian port city, which was a beautiful and challenging task. Early on, we hand-stowed lumber piece by piece, and later mechanization made the job easier. That city was one of the ports where I experienced the variety and evolution of waterfront work firsthand.

explicit_text: Larry Clark was a good friend and someone I admired greatly. He encouraged me to transfer to Local 40 in the late 1990s, which was a good move for me to learn another side of the waterfront. Thanks to Larry's support, I expanded my union involvement and eventually served as president and dispatcher at Local 40 until I retired.
